title: Rolled Ankles: What to Do
summary: Learn to spot a rolled ankle, what to do right away, and when it needs a doctor instead of just rest.

sections.heading: Why this matters
body: A rolled ankle — doctors call it an ankle sprain — is the most common injury in youth sports. It happens in basketball, soccer, volleyball, football, and just about every other sport that involves running, jumping, or cutting.
You don't need to be a doctor to help. Your job is to notice it happened, know the basic first steps, and know when to get an adult right away.

sections.heading: What's actually happening
body: Your ankle is held together by ligaments — strong bands of tissue that connect bone to bone, kind of like sturdy rubber straps. They keep your ankle from moving too far in any one direction.

When your foot rolls or twists too hard — usually inward, so the bottom of your foot turns toward your other foot — those ligaments get stretched too far or torn. That stretch or tear is what we call a sprain.

image.description: Simple side view of an ankle showing the ligaments as sturdy strap-like bands connecting the shin bone and foot bone. Highlight one ligament in red on the outside of the ankle, shown stretched and slightly torn, to illustrate what a sprain is. Clean, simple illustration style for middle schoolers, minimal labels.
image.alt: Simple diagram of ankle ligaments, with one shown stretched and torn on the outside of the ankle.

sections.heading: How to spot it
body: Most rolled ankles look and feel similar. Watch for:
list: The player rolls, twists, or 'turns over' their ankle, often landing on someone's foot or an uneven spot
Pain right around the ankle, usually worse on the outside
Swelling — the ankle starts to puff up, sometimes within minutes
Bruising that shows up later (not always right away)
Limping, favoring the ankle, or not wanting to put weight on it

image.description: Side-by-side illustration comparing a normal ankle and a sprained ankle with visible puffy swelling and light bruising around the outside bone. Simple, clean line-and-color style appropriate for middle schoolers, no gore.
image.alt: Comparison drawing of a normal ankle next to a swollen, bruised sprained ankle.

sections.heading: What to do right away
body: If you see or suspect a rolled ankle, follow these steps in order:
list: Stop playing. Don't try to 'walk it off' or finish the play.
Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or other trusted adult right away.
Rest — stay off the ankle and let the adult decide next steps.
Ice — a bag of ice wrapped in a thin towel, about 15-20 minutes, never straight on bare skin.
Elevate — prop the ankle up higher than the heart when sitting or lying down, if possible.

image.description: Simple illustration of an athlete sitting on a bench with an ice pack wrapped in a thin towel on their ankle, the leg propped up on a pillow above heart level. Clean, friendly illustration style, no text in the image.
image.alt: Athlete sitting with an ice pack on a wrapped ankle propped up above heart level.

sections.heading: Red flags — this might be more than a sprain
body: Most rolled ankles are simple sprains that get better with rest. But some signs mean the bone itself might be broken, not just the ligaments stretched. These need a doctor, not just rest and ice.

callout.title: Get an adult and see a doctor if you notice:
callout.text: The athlete cannot put any weight on the foot at all (not even a little), the ankle or foot looks bent or crooked (deformity), the foot feels numb or looks pale/cold, or the pain is severe and not improving. Any of these means: stop, tell an adult immediately, and get it checked by a doctor. Never wait and see with these signs.

image.description: Side-by-side illustration comparing a normal ankle to one with an obvious bent or crooked deformity, drawn simply with a warning-colored outline around the injured ankle to signal danger. Clean, non-graphic style for middle schoolers.
image.alt: Comparison drawing of a normal ankle next to a bent, deformed ankle marked as a warning sign.

sections.heading: Getting back to sport
body: Don't decide on your own when a teammate (or you) can go back to play — that call belongs to an athletic trainer, coach following medical guidance, or a doctor.

Going back too soon, before the ankle is fully healed, is one of the biggest reasons athletes re-sprain the same ankle again and again. Healing takes patience, even when a sprain feels 'not that bad.'

quiz.question: What is a ligament?
choices: A strong band of tissue that connects bone to bone
A type of muscle in the calf
The bone on the outside of the ankle
A brace worn during sports
quiz.explanation: Ligaments connect bone to bone and hold joints like the ankle together. When they stretch or tear too far, that's a sprain.

quiz.question: Which direction does the foot usually roll in a typical ankle sprain?
choices: Straight up
Inward, so the sole turns toward the other foot
It doesn't matter, sprains don't involve rolling
Backward, like stepping off a curb
quiz.explanation: Most ankle sprains happen when the foot rolls inward, stretching or tearing the ligaments on the outside of the ankle.

quiz.question: A teammate rolls her ankle during a game. What should you do first?
choices: Tell her to keep playing if she can handle the pain
Wrap it with tape yourself so she can finish the game
Have her stop playing and tell a coach or athletic trainer right away
Tell her to stretch it out
quiz.explanation: The first step is always to stop playing and get a trusted adult involved. Students should never tape an injury or decide someone can keep playing.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT one of the immediate first-aid steps for a simple rolled ankle?
choices: Rest
Ice
Elevate
Massage it hard to work out the swelling
quiz.explanation: Rest, ice, and elevation help a sprain. Massaging or pushing on a fresh injury can make swelling and pain worse and is not a first-aid step.

quiz.question: How should ice be applied to a rolled ankle?
choices: Directly on bare skin for an hour
Wrapped in a thin towel, about 15-20 minutes at a time
Only after the swelling has already gone away
Ice should never be used on a sprain
quiz.explanation: Ice should go over a thin towel, not bare skin, for about 15-20 minutes at a time. This cools the area without causing an ice burn.

quiz.question: Which sign is a red flag that means an athlete needs to see a doctor right away, not just rest?
choices: Mild swelling around the ankle bone
Complete inability to put any weight on the foot at all
Some soreness the next morning
Wanting to sit out the rest of practice
quiz.explanation: Being unable to bear any weight at all — along with deformity, numbness, or coldness in the foot — suggests a possible broken bone, which needs a doctor right away.

quiz.question: Why is it risky for an athlete to go back to play too soon after a rolled ankle?
choices: It isn't risky — sprains heal instantly once the pain stops
It can lead to re-spraining the same ankle again
It causes a completely different injury to the knee
It only matters for professional athletes
quiz.explanation: Returning before a sprain has fully healed is one of the top reasons athletes hurt the same ankle again. Return-to-play decisions belong to a trained professional.

quiz.question: Who should decide when an athlete is ready to return to play after a rolled ankle?
choices: The athlete themselves, based on how tough they feel
A teammate who has had a similar injury before
An athletic trainer, doctor, or coach following medical guidance
Whoever is closest to the field at the time
quiz.explanation: Return-to-play decisions should always come from an athletic trainer, doctor, or coach acting on medical guidance — never a self-decision or a guess by a peer.

flashcards.back: When the ligaments holding the ankle together get stretched or torn, usually from the foot rolling or twisting.
A strong band of tissue that connects bone to bone and holds a joint together.

flashcards.front: Most common direction of ankle roll
flashcards.back: Inward — the sole of the foot turns toward the other foot.
Pain, swelling, bruising (sometimes later), and trouble putting weight on the foot.

flashcards.front: First step after a rolled ankle
flashcards.back: Stop playing right away — don't try to walk it off or finish the play.

flashcards.front: Second step after a rolled ankle
flashcards.back: Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or other trusted adult immediately.
The basic first-aid steps for a simple rolled ankle: stay off it, ice it, and prop it up above the heart.
Wrap ice in a thin towel (never bare skin) and apply for about 15-20 minutes at a time.

flashcards.front: Red flag: can't bear any weight
flashcards.back: If someone cannot put any weight at all on the foot, it might be broken, not just sprained — get a doctor right away.
If the ankle or foot looks bent or crooked, this is a sign of a possible fracture — get medical help immediately.

flashcards.front: Other red flags to watch for
flashcards.back: Numbness, coldness, or pale color in the foot, or severe pain that isn't improving.

flashcards.front: Who decides when to return to play?
flashcards.back: An athletic trainer, doctor, or coach following medical guidance — never the athlete or a teammate alone.

sources.title: National Athletic Trainers' Association Position Statement: Conservative Management and Prevention of Ankle Sprains in Athletes
